description: Principles of non-maleficence: the duty to avoid causing harm across four recognized dimensions (physical, psychological, economic, social) plus the duty to avoid contributing to indirect harm. Grounded in: Beauchamp & Childress, Principles of Biomedical Ethics (8th ed., 2019) Ch. 5 — paraphrase only, NOT verbatim text. Also informed by UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation (2021) §§33-36.

entries.statement: Any action that causes or knowingly risks bodily injury, physical pain, or impairment of physical health must be avoided.
entries.source: Beauchamp & Childress, Principles of Biomedical Ethics (8th ed.) Ch. 5 §1 (paraphrase)

entries.statement: Actions that cause emotional distress, psychological trauma, humiliation, or deliberate damage to a person's mental wellbeing must be avoided.
entries.source: Beauchamp & Childress, Principles of Biomedical Ethics (8th ed.) Ch. 5 §2 (paraphrase)

entries.statement: Actions that knowingly expose persons to financial loss, fraud, exploitation, or economic deprivation must be avoided.
entries.source: Beauchamp & Childress, Principles of Biomedical Ethics (8th ed.) Ch. 5 §3 (paraphrase); UNESCO AI Ethics (2021) §34

entries.statement: Actions that damage a person's social relationships, reputation, standing in a community, or access to social goods must be avoided.
entries.source: Beauchamp & Childress, Principles of Biomedical Ethics (8th ed.) Ch. 5 §4 (paraphrase)

entries.statement: Even when an action does not directly harm, contributing to chains of events that foreseeably cause harm to others — including through amplifying conflicts or providing means for harm — is itself a violation of the duty of non-maleficence.
entries.source: Beauchamp & Childress, Principles of Biomedical Ethics (8th ed.) Ch. 5 §5 (paraphrase); UNESCO AI Ethics (2021) §35
